summary: Gruber highlights polling data shared by Benedict Evans showing that smartphones have overtaken laptops as the device people consider most important for connecting to the internet. In just two years (2013-2015), smartphones went from 15% to 33%, while laptops dropped from 46% to 30%. Gruber regroups the data into 'mobile' (phone + tablet) versus 'PC' (laptop + desktop) categories, showing mobile has decisively surpassed PC. He acknowledges the grouping isn't perfectly rigorous given hybrid devices like Surface and Chromebooks, but argues the dominance of iOS and Android makes it a fair comparison. His conclusion is succinct: the post-PC world has arrived.
key_points: Smartphones surpassed laptops as the most important internet device in just two years, going from 15% to 33%
When grouped as mobile vs PC, mobile devices (52%) have clearly overtaken traditional PCs (44%) by 2015
The mobile/PC distinction conflates form factor with OS, but iOS and Android dominance makes it a useful approximation
Chromebooks complicate the categorization — not truly mobile, but clearly part of the post-PC disruption
Gruber personally finds both Mac and iPhone indispensable, suggesting the transition is felt even by power users
money_quotes: Assuming the polling is valid, this suggests we've already passed the inflection point where most people consider their mobile devices (phone and tablet) central to their use of the internet.
The bottom line: the post-PC world is here.
It's probably wrong to say Chrome is a 'mobile' OS, but it certainly isn't a traditional PC platform.
What I'm interested in is the post-PC disruption of the industry, and Chromebooks are clearly a part of that, even if they're instantiated in a very traditional laptop form factor.
A Mac and an iPhone both feel indispensable to me. If I really had to make do with just one, I suppose I'd pick a MacBook, but that's not the question that was asked.
In terms of my actual usage, my iPhone might be 'the most important device I use to connect to the internet'.
key_insight: By 2015, the inflection point had already passed — most people considered mobile devices more important than PCs for internet use, confirming the post-PC era Steve Jobs predicted.
